event: God Disperses the People at the Tower of Babel
symbols: God appearing in glory above — divine intervention to halt human hubris
scattered and fleeing peoples — the confusion of languages and dispersal of the nations
diverse groups moving in different directions — the origin of the world's linguistic and ethnic diversity
heavenly radiance — the overwhelming presence of God descending to judge
central figure in white — possibly a patriarch or leader amid the confusion
animals and possessions scattered — the disruption of the unified human enterprise
description: A large sepia-toned engraving or print showing a dramatic scene with a radiant divine figure appearing in glory at the top center, surrounded by clouds and light. Below, a vast crowd of people in various states of agitation, confusion, and flight spreads across the landscape. Groups move in all directions, some carrying possessions and animals. The central area shows figures in classical robes looking upward in awe or terror. The composition is monumental and sweeping, with the divine appearance creating a focal point above the human chaos below.
theological_description: The Tower of Babel narrative (GEN.11.1-9) describes humanity's attempt to build a city and tower 'with its top in the heavens' to 'make a name for ourselves, lest we be dispersed over the face of the whole earth' (GEN.11.4). God responds by confusing their language and scattering them — the very outcome they feared. The episode reveals the pattern of human hubris meeting divine judgment that runs through Genesis: like the Fall and the Flood before it, Babel demonstrates that human autonomy apart from God leads to fragmentation and confusion. The Fathers saw Babel as the anti-type of Pentecost (ACT.2.1-11), where the confusion of tongues is reversed as the Holy Spirit enables people of every nation to hear the Gospel in their own language. The scattering of the nations at Babel thus becomes, in God's providence, the preparation for the global mission of the Church. God's descent to 'see the city and the tower' (GEN.11.5) is an ironic anthropomorphism: the tower that was to reach heaven was so insignificant that God had to come down to see it.
